meta.name: Read the Room — PI Question Bank
meta.source: Derived from The Predictive Index People Data Toolkit (concepts paraphrased, original wording)

schema.options: array of exactly 4 answer strings (map to the 4 colored tiles)
schema.teach: one-line explainer shown after the reveal

meta.note: Extend by following the same schema. Keep 4 options per question so the Kahoot-style 2x2 color grid always works.

questions.q: Factor A (Dominance) is the drive to…
options: Exert your influence on people and events
questions.teach: Dominance = the drive to take charge and shape outcomes.

questions.q: Factor B (Extraversion) is the drive for…
questions.teach: Extraversion = the drive for social connection and influence.

questions.q: Factor C (Patience) is the drive for…
questions.teach: Patience = the drive for steadiness, calm, and a consistent pace.

questions.q: Factor D (Formality) is the drive to…
questions.teach: Formality = the drive to do it 'by the book' with accuracy and structure.

questions.q: Which set of behaviors describes someone HIGH in Dominance?
questions.teach: High A shows up as independent, assertive, and challenging.

questions.q: Which describes someone LOW in Dominance?
questions.teach: Low A is collaborative, supportive, and accommodating.

questions.q: A person HIGH in Extraversion tends to be…
questions.teach: High B is sociable, animated, and convincing.

questions.q: Which describes someone LOW in Extraversion?
questions.teach: Low B is introspective, private, and prefers facts over chit-chat.

questions.q: Someone HIGH in Patience is usually…
questions.teach: High C is peaceful, steady, and comfortable with the familiar.

questions.q: A LOW Patience person is more likely to be…
questions.teach: Low C is intense, brisk, and impatient with routine.

questions.q: Which describes someone HIGH in Formality?
questions.teach: High D is precise, disciplined, and does it the 'correct' way.

questions.q: “Rules are really just guidelines.” That attitude points to…
questions.teach: Low D is flexible, casual, and comfortable with ambiguity.

questions.q: A HIGH Dominance person is most motivated by…
questions.teach: High A wants independence, a challenge, and to be recognized individually.

questions.q: Which motivates a LOW Dominance person?
options: Collaboration and freedom from individual competition
questions.teach: Low A is energized by collaboration, reassurance, and shared wins.

questions.q: A HIGH Extraversion person is motivated by…
options: Public recognition and connection with others
questions.teach: High B wants interaction, social acceptance, and visible accomplishment.

questions.q: Which motivates a LOW Extraversion person?
options: Privacy and time to trust others
questions.teach: Low B wants room to reflect, private recognition, and work with facts.

questions.q: A HIGH Patience person is motivated by…
options: A stable environment and steady pace
questions.teach: High C wants stability, familiar surroundings, and recognition for loyalty.

questions.q: Which motivates a LOW Patience person?
options: Variety, change, and a faster pace
A single priority at a time
questions.teach: Low C wants variety, mobility, and freedom from repetition.

questions.q: A HIGH Formality person is motivated by…
questions.teach: High D wants specific knowledge, clear rules, and freedom from error.

questions.q: Which motivates a LOW Formality person?
options: Flexibility and freedom from rigid structure
questions.teach: Low D wants flexibility, informality, and room to be spontaneous.

questions.q: When Dominance is higher than Extraversion (A > B), the person tends to be…
questions.teach: A > B = task-oriented: critical thinker, problem solver, holds authority tightly.

questions.q: When Extraversion is higher than Dominance (B > A), the person is usually…
questions.teach: B > A = people-oriented: collaborative, delegates easily, comfortable on a team.

questions.q: A person with A > C (Dominance over Patience) is best described as…
questions.teach: A > C = proactive: takes initiative, thrives under pressure, impatient with routine.

questions.q: Someone with C > A (Patience over Dominance) is more…
questions.teach: C > A = responsive: consistent, cooperative, takes action carefully.

questions.q: A > D (Dominance over Formality) signals someone who is…
questions.teach: A > D = comfortable with risk: independent, venturesome, sees risk as opportunity.

questions.q: D > A (Formality over Dominance) points to someone who is…
questions.teach: D > A = cautious with risk: careful, conservative, needs clarity around rules.

questions.q: B > C (Extraversion over Patience) describes someone who is…
options: Quiet and slow to open up
questions.teach: B > C = quick to connect: lively, enthusiastic, builds relationships fast.

questions.q: C > B (Patience over Extraversion) means a person who…
options: Takes time to connect and thinks before speaking
questions.teach: C > B = takes time to connect: reserved, organizes thoughts before expressing them.

questions.q: B > D (Extraversion over Formality) tends to be…
questions.teach: B > D = informal: shares freely, processes out loud, delegates details easily.

questions.q: D > B (Formality over Extraversion) describes someone who is…
questions.teach: D > B = formal: disciplined, selective in what they share, cautious with new people.

questions.q: C > D (Patience over Formality) is someone who is…
options: Casual with rules and comfortable with ambiguity
questions.teach: C > D = casual with rules: relaxed, persistent, treats rules as general guidelines.

questions.q: D > C (Formality over Patience) points to someone who is…
options: Careful with rules and strong on follow-up
questions.teach: D > C = careful with rules: conscientious, precise, strict about correctness.

questions.q: In a factor combination, what makes the relationship express MORE strongly?
options: A narrower gap between the two factors
A wider spread between the two factors
Both factors being near the midpoint
questions.teach: The wider the spread (especially across the midpoint), the stronger the expression.

questions.q: Which Reference Profile is the bold, outside-the-box innovator who needs freedom from rules?
questions.teach: Maverick — venturesome, animated, quick to act, delegates freely with loose follow-up.

questions.q: Which profile is a results-oriented leader who wants others to buy into their ideas?
questions.teach: Captain — problem-solver, confident risk-taker, drives the big picture.

questions.q: Warmth, charm, and a persuasive selling style point to which profile?
questions.teach: Persuader — team-builder, rallies people around goals, strong ego and initiative.

questions.q: Warm, patient team player who is most effective with the familiar and dislikes risk?
questions.teach: Collaborator — friendly, steady, helpful, a good listener who avoids risk.

questions.q: A complete extravert who sells intangibles and won't take no for an answer is a…
questions.teach: Promoter — casual, verbal, effective with groups, delegates with little follow-up.

questions.q: A self-starting, independent generalist and risk-taker (A > B, low C) is a…
questions.teach: Venturer — results-driven, freedom from structure, creative troubleshooter.

questions.q: Intensely analytical and risk-averse, wanting all the answers before acting, describes the…
questions.teach: Analyzer — thorough, disciplined, skeptical; second-guesses and follows up tightly.

questions.q: Reserved, precise, respects authority, prefers specifics over ambiguity — which profile?
questions.teach: Specialist — needs strong structure, high-precision detail work, cautious communication.

questions.q: Thorough, accurate, technically expert and self-reliant (A > B, high C) is a…
questions.teach: Scholar — methodical, introspective, patient; tight delegation with strong follow-up.

questions.q: Precise detail worker who needs structure and works harmoniously with the group is a…
questions.teach: Guardian — adheres to the rules, patient, unselfish, opens up once familiar.

questions.q: Factor E measures the extent to which a person, at the point of decision, tends to be…
questions.teach: Factor E: subjective (gut/feelings, below midpoint) vs. objective (facts/logic).

questions.q: In the PI pattern, the Self represents…
options: How a person adapts to the current environment
A person's basic, natural motivations and needs
questions.teach: Self = what comes naturally. Self-Concept = how they think they must adapt.

questions.q: The Synthesis pattern is best described as…
options: How a person usually behaves at work (Self + Self-Concept)
questions.teach: Synthesis = the average of Self and Self-Concept — how the person typically shows up at work.

questions.q: Factor M represents…
options: The number of checklist words a person selected
questions.teach: Factor M = how many words were chosen. Very low M (<10) makes extreme patterns unreliable.

questions.q: As a factor moves farther from the midpoint, the associated behavior becomes…
questions.teach: Distance from the midpoint = intensity. Farther out means a stronger expression of the drive.

questions.q: A best practice when reading results back to someone is to…
options: Use plain language and describe the person, not the jargon
Read out the High-A / Low-B labels
questions.teach: Stay neutral, describe a person (not a pattern), start with strengths, skip the jargon.

questions.q: On the PI Behavioral Assessment, the correct answer set is…
options: There are no right or wrong answers
questions.teach: The Behavioral Assessment has no right or wrong answers — and no time limit.

questions.q: The PI Cognitive Assessment is…
options: A 12-minute, 50-question test of general cognitive ability
questions.teach: The CA is a 12-minute, 50-question measure of general cognitive ability ('g').

questions.q: The Cognitive Assessment measures 'g' using which three content categories?
questions.teach: The CA uses verbal, numerical, and abstract reasoning to measure general cognitive ability.

questions.q: A candidate's CA match score runs from 1–10, where 10 means…
options: The ideal match to the cognitive level the job requires
questions.teach: The 1–10 match score compares the candidate's CA result to the role's cognitive target.
